questions.question: Architecture: A single load balancer terminates TLS and forwards to 50 stateless app servers. All app servers connect to one primary MySQL instance (no replicas). What is the most critical flaw?
options: The database is a single point of failure—its outage takes down the entire service despite scaled app tier
Stateless app servers cannot handle HTTP requests
TLS termination on a load balancer is always invalid
Fifty app servers is too many for any workload
questions.explanation: Scaling the app tier does not help if all state lives in one DB with no failover. The SPOF dominates availability. LB TLS termination and server count are distractors.

questions.question: Architecture: A social feed service shards posts by user_id across 100 databases. Celebrity users with millions of followers have all fan-out reads hitting their home shard. What flaw will appear first at scale?
options: Hot partition / skewed shard bottleneck on high-follower users despite uniform sharding by user_id
Too many databases—100 shards is always excessive
Sharding by user_id prevents any reads from working
Fan-out reads cannot exist in social networks
questions.explanation: Uniform hash/user sharding does not solve hot keys. Celebrity traffic concentrates on one shard, causing latency spikes—a classic bottleneck distractors ignore.

questions.question: Architecture: Writes update the database synchronously but cache invalidation is async via a queue with multi-second lag. Reads always go to cache first. What user-visible bug is most likely?
options: Stale reads—clients see outdated data for seconds after a successful write
Lost writes because caches cannot store strings
Duplicate primary keys in the database
TLS handshake failures between cache and clients
questions.explanation: Read-through cache with delayed invalidation creates a consistency window where users read old values after updates. This is a classic cache coherency flaw.

questions.question: Architecture: A payment pipeline with 12 microservices, each logging locally to disk. No centralized metrics, tracing, or SLO dashboards. On-call relies on customer complaints. What critical flaw is missing?
options: Observability—you cannot detect degradation or localize failures before users report them
Too many microservices—12 is always wrong
Local disk logs are illegal in distributed systems
Payments cannot be implemented with microservices
questions.explanation: Without metrics, tracing, or alerting, operators lack detection—violating Google's 'how do we know the system is working' criterion. Service count alone is not inherently fatal.

questions.question: Architecture: Global service uses one DNS A record pointing to a single anycast IP in one region. No health checks or failover DNS. The region suffers a network partition. What happens?
options: Total outage for users worldwide until manual DNS change—regional SPOF at the routing layer
Automatic failover to other regions because anycast always heals partitions
Only users in that region are affected; DNS geo-routes automatically without configuration
Clients retry until the partition heals with no user impact
questions.explanation: Single-region DNS with no failover creates a global SPOF. Anycast does not magically solve partition without multi-region deployment and health-checked routing.

questions.question: Architecture: Video uploads go to API servers that synchronously transcode 4K video before returning HTTP 200. Average transcode takes 8 minutes. Traffic is growing 10×. What bottleneck breaks first?
options: Synchronous long-running work ties up API workers and destroys throughput/latency under load
HTTP cannot transfer video files
4K transcoding is impossible on servers
Growing traffic automatically speeds up transcoding
questions.explanation: Blocking API threads on minutes-long work creates a severe bottleneck. The fix is async job queues and polling/webhooks—not faster CPUs alone.

questions.question: Architecture: Two datacenters each run a cron job nightly to aggregate billing. Both read the same DB and write totals without coordination or idempotency keys. Clock skew can cause both to run 'simultaneously.' What bug appears?
options: Double-counting or conflicting writes—lack of distributed coordination/idempotency causes inconsistent aggregates
Billing systems cannot use cron jobs
Clock skew prevents databases from starting
Aggregates become more accurate when duplicated
questions.explanation: Uncoordinated duplicate workers produce consistency bugs (double processing). Needs leader election, distributed locks, or idempotent write semantics.

questions.question: Architecture: A read-heavy product catalog serves 500K QPS from a single PostgreSQL primary with no read replicas and no cache. All queries are simple primary-key lookups. What fails first?
options: The database becomes the throughput bottleneck—500K QPS exceeds practical single-node read capacity
Primary-key lookups are illegal in PostgreSQL
Read-heavy workloads require graph databases only
500K QPS is trivial for any laptop running Postgres
questions.explanation: Even simple queries at hundreds of thousands of QPS overwhelm one DB node. Caching and read replicas address read scaling—a missing layer here.

questions.question: Architecture: An event bus ingests spikes to 1M events/sec. Consumers lag silently with no queue depth metrics, consumer lag alerts, or dead-letter queues. Producers have unlimited retry with no backoff. What combined flaw is most dangerous?
options: Missing observability plus retry storms—operators cannot see backlog growth and retries amplify overload
Event buses cannot handle more than 1000 events/sec by definition
Dead-letter queues are only for email systems
Producers should never retry failed sends
questions.explanation: Invisible consumer lag plus aggressive retries creates cascading failure (retry storm). Observability (lag metrics, DLQ) and backpressure are essential—not optional polish.

questions.question: Architecture: User profile updates write to the primary DB in us-east. Reads immediately after write go to async replicas in us-west with up to 30s replication lag. The app routes reads to nearest replica for latency. What user-visible flaw occurs?
options: Read-your-writes violation—users in us-west may not see their update for up to 30 seconds
Async replication is impossible across regions
Nearest-replica routing always guarantees strong consistency
Profile updates cannot be stored in relational databases
questions.explanation: Geo-routed reads from lagging async replicas break read-your-writes consistency after updates. Fixes include sticky routing to primary post-write or synchronous/quorum reads for recent writes.
